region_of_origin: Native to Central America; major commercial production in Vietnam, Malaysia, and Thailand; widely sold in Singapore
food_subgroup: Fresh tropical fruit — pink leathery skin and green-tipped scales removed; white flesh with embedded black seeds consumed raw

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific term for Hylocereus undatus (white-flesh dragon fruit). Closest parent concept is tropical cactus fruit. No URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pitahaya (dragon fruit) — FoodEx2 group A00LC covers cactus pear and pitahaya under the broader tropical and subtropical fruits category
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC is the closest defensible parent for pitahaya/dragon fruit; no species-level code for Hylocereus undatus white-flesh variant exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for dragon fruit / Hylocereus undatus as a food item in the international release.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Singapore serving is half a commercially sold medium dragon fruit, yielding approximately 170g of edible white flesh after the pink skin and green-tipped scales are removed.

nutrient_source.title: Dragon Fruit (Pitaya), raw — USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient database
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central) and Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for white-flesh dragon fruit (Hylocereus undatus) sourced from USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo. Values represent raw edible flesh per 100g. Calcium value of 107mg/100g is notably high and consistent with published USDA data for this species.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of dragon fruit estimated at approximately 45 — analogous estimate based on FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia food composition data and published peer-reviewed literature on tropical fruit GI values; direct GI measurement for Hylocereus undatus is limited in the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate); FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia
gi_source.source_match_notes: Published GI studies specifically for white-flesh dragon fruit (Hylocereus undatus) are limited. GI ~45 is an analogous estimate based on the fruit's macronutrient profile (moderate carbohydrate 13g/100g, high fibre 3g/100g, low sugar 8g/100g) and comparison with similarly profiled tropical fruits in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 170g serving = (45 × 13 × 1.70) / 100 ≈ 9.9. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate potassium (236mg/100g) with relatively low sodium (39mg/100g); notably high calcium (107mg/100g) for a fruit
hypertension.guidance: Dragon fruit is suitable for hypertensive patients — its potassium-to-sodium ratio supports blood pressure regulation, and its unusually high calcium content (107mg/100g, comparable to some dairy products) may provide additional cardiovascular benefit; a 170g serving contributes approximately 401mg potassium and 182mg calcium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~45), moderate GL per serving (~10), high dietary fibre (3g/100g), and prebiotic fructooligosaccharides
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dragon fruit is one of the better everyday fruit choices for patients with Type 2 Diabetes — its low GI (~45), moderate glycemic load per half-fruit serving (~10), high fibre content (3g/100g) slowing glucose absorption, and prebiotic oligosaccharides that may improve gut microbiome composition and insulin sensitivity make it a clinically appropriate inclusion in a T2DM dietary plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero cholesterol, negligible saturated fat, high dietary fibre, and antioxidant betalain pigments
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dragon fruit is cardioprotective — it contains zero cholesterol, negligible saturated fat (0g/100g), 3g/100g dietary fibre supporting LDL reduction, and betalain antioxidants (betacyanins) in the pink skin and flesh that have demonstrated anti-inflammatory and lipid-lowering properties in emerging research; the embedded black seeds also contribute a small amount of omega-3 ALA.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Betalain antioxidants, omega-3 ALA from seeds, and iron (0.65mg/100g) relevant for RA-associated anaemia
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dragon fruit may benefit rheumatoid arthritis patients through its betalain pigments with anti-inflammatory properties, omega-3 ALA from embedded black seeds, and iron content (0.65mg/100g) that is relevant for the anaemia of chronic inflammation commonly seen in RA patients on long-term NSAID therapy.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dragon Fruit with white flesh (Hylocereus undatus), also known as pitaya or 火龙果 (Huǒ Lóng Guǒ) / buah naga, is one of Singapore's most visually striking supermarket fruits. It has a hot-pink leathery skin with green-tipped scale-like protrusions, and snow-white flesh densely speckled with tiny edible black seeds. Originally native to Central America, it is now mass-produced in Vietnam, Thailand, and Malaysia and is ubiquitous in Singapore supermarkets year-round. The white-flesh variety (Hylocereus undatus) is the most commonly sold in Singapore; the red-flesh variety (Hylocereus polyrhizus) is also available and has significantly higher betalain (betacyanin) antioxidant content. The flavour of white-flesh dragon fruit is mild and mildly sweet — it lacks the intense character of durian, mango, or rambutan, and its visual drama is its primary appeal. It is typically eaten raw, scooped from the halved fruit or sliced. Nutritionally, dragon fruit is genuinely beneficial: low-medium GI (~45), high dietary fibre (3g/100g), notably high calcium (107mg/100g — comparable to some dairy products on a per-100g basis), good potassium (236mg/100g), and prebiotic fructooligosaccharides that support gut microbiome health. It is low in calories (60 kcal/100g), contains no cholesterol, and has negligible saturated fat. It is one of the better everyday fruit choices for patients with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. A standard Singapore serving is half a medium fruit (~170g flesh), providing approximately 102 kcal, 22g carbohydrate, 5.1g fibre, and a glycemic load of approximately 10.
common_user_queries: white vs red dragon fruit nutrition
is dragon fruit good for diabetes

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo — both approved primary sources. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence 0.9) due to limited direct GI studies for Hylocereus undatus, but is well-supported by the fruit's macronutrient profile and comparison with analogous tropical fruits in the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for dragon fruit white flesh is not reported in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore Foodo as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the primary sources consulted. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central (naturally zero for this fruit). cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero — naturally zero as this is a plant food. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for white-flesh variety. vitamin_b12_mcg is naturally zero — plant food with no animal-derived components. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. GI value of ~45 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement studies for Hylocereus undatus white-flesh are limited in the published literature. The calcium value of 107mg/100g is notably high for a fruit and is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for this species. The red-flesh variety (Hylocereus polyrhizus) has significantly higher betalain antioxidant content and is preferable for patients seeking maximum anti-inflammatory benefit.
